data.title: CLSE: Long/Short Fund Ideal For Today's Macro Set-Up (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

We have covered the Convergence Long/Short Equity ETF (CLSE) before, namely in 2024, when we outlined the general set-up of this equities exchange-traded fund and its unique long/short strategy.

With the equity market stretched, in our opinion, we are going to revisit the name and its analytics and outline why structurally CLSE represents a better alternative to being long equities outright.

The value proposition in long-short funds

Let us first take a look at CLSE's objective:

The fund endeavors to provide a more material and consistent alpha through its proprietary fundamental ranking process from both its long and short holdings over a market cycle. The objective of the Convergence Long/Short Equity ETF is to pursue long-term capital growth while minimizing volatility.

As opposed to long-only names, CLSE also aims to minimize volatility, and it does so via its construction. Let us look at related analytics:

The fund uses the Russell 3000 as a benchmark and has a standard deviation that is 30% lower when compared to the long-only Russell. The name is more volatile than a Long/Short Morningstar index, which posted an 8.6% standard deviation. The fund, however, used its volatility smartly, showing a high Sharpe ratio of 0.95 for the risk it took. Smart risk taking is also illustrated via its high alpha ratio and a beta which is only half the index figure.

The ETF is net long as of the latest disclosure:

The fund's long exposure clocks in at 116%, while its short exposure is -52%. Let us look at how the parsing shapes-up on a sectoral basis:

The fund is overweight net long information technology, financials, and healthcare. The name is not outright short any of the sectors currently. From a fundamental perspective, the ETF tends to be net long equities with lower P/E ratios, and short more speculative names with very high P/E figures:

On the long side the average P/E ratio is 24x, whereas on the short side the average P/E is 460x. Similarly, the short book is made up of high beta names (average of 1.38) versus a .99 beta on the long side. The manager is long fundamentally sound names, whilst short more speculative equities. This setup ensures that in a down market, the high beta equities provide for a significant cushion versus the market P/E ones. We have seen this picture before in 2022.

Drawdowns and annual returns

Let us have a look at how the name did during the 2022 market downturn:

The name began trading as an ETF in February 2022 (it was a mutual fund before), hence the issues with some data platforms. Its drawdown was only -12%, very similar to what the First Trust Long/Short Equity ETF (FTLS) posted. We overlaid here the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) and the iShares Russell 3000 ETF (IWV).

While its downside was limited in 2022, what is more impressive about CLSE is its ability to capture the upside. In fact, the name has done extremely well versus the index, explaining the monster 8.8% Alpha figure we saw above. The portfolio manager here is very apt at identifying momentum names and riding the strongest candidates in a bull market. On the chosen 3-year time-frame, CLSE is up +48%, trouncing both the SPY and the IWV. The takeaway here for an investor is that long-short funds do not necessarily lag during recoveries, and CLSE is a prime example of an alpha generator.

From a historic standpoint, let us also have a look at how the name did throughout the years:

During overall market downturns, the fund has low negative total returns, such as the ones observed in 2015, 2018, and 2020. 2022 is a bit of an exception, with CLSE posting a higher negative total return versus its peers in the long-short space, but nonetheless a very respectable figure versus -18% for the SPY and -19% for IWV.

Expect a similar performance during the next drawdown. History has shown us that the portfolio manager is dynamic and agile in identifying trends and adjusting the portfolio to minimize down moves.

The macro picture is full of red flags

While nobody can time the market, the overall market valuation levels are currently stretched:

When looking back a decade, we can see the average P/E level lies somewhere around the 17x figure, while the top of the range is 22x and the bottom is 14x. We are currently at the top of the range. While market momentum can continue upwards, it is only fair to say that the market is stretched. When and if it will move lower, we do not know. History has taught us, though, that mean reversion happens over multiple cycles, and stretched valuations cannot stay high for too long.

We are not advocating for a recession here or a market crash. We are simply pointing out that the fundamental valuation metrics of equities are stretched, and at some point they will revert. When that mean reversion does happen, it could be swift and violent.

CLSE has proven to be a very smooth operator in a bull market, outperforming both SPY and IWV; thus, an investor here is not going to be long a low reward fund. Moreover, the name has shallow, well-defined drawdowns in bear markets, mainly helped by its short position in very high P/E names. In a bear market, names that go up based on euphoria tend to fall very hard, thus providing a windfall for the fund. In our view, CLSE is superior to outright SPY or IWV in today's market.

Conclusion

CLSE is a long-short equity fund. The name is net long per sector but holds long positions in equities with a 24x average P/E ratio, while the short book sees average P/E levels of 460x. The fund had low negative total returns during bear markets and has outperformed both SPY and IWV in the past two years, proving its alpha generation capabilities in a bull market. With overall equities stretched, we favor a rotation out of SPY and IWV into a fund like CLSE.

data.title: Google's AI Search Supremacy Won't Guarantee Market-Beating Returns
data.description:
Since my last Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) (TSX:GOOG:CA) analysis, in which I assigned a Hold rating, the stock has declined by -7% in price. That Q4 preview assessed all around strength in Google's Advertising, Search, and Cloud segments. However, the company's perceptibly most vulnerable segment, Search, may have more resilience and potential for accretion for the company in the long term than currently broadly acknowledged by the market. While models like OpenAI's ChatGPT Search, Meta's (META) Llama search potential, and new novel AI variants like DeepSeek-R1 and xAI models show a growing competitive landscape, Google has a unique position to trade on with a first-mover advantage in high-consumer-access internet indexing. Therefore, with the right strategy and execution, Google could emerge as the leader in AI-powered internet navigation—put simply, with the power of Gemini and the foundation of Google Search, an intuitive, consumer-friendly, and redesigned AI-powered Search could set Google miles apart from competitors. The chance of moat preservation remains heightened while the company still has the dominant front page of the internet through Google.com (it hasn't yet lost this to ChatGPT or other AI model developers en masse). However, investors have to contend with the reality that Google's growth is limited due to market saturation factors and increasing competitive pressures.

Q4 Overview

Google's Q4 results were marked by strength in Google Services, where revenue grew by 10%, and remarkable progress in Google Cloud, where revenue grew by 30%, driven by Google Cloud Platform products, AI infrastructure, and Generative AI solutions. The company's total net income grew by 28%, and capital expenditures ('capex') for Fiscal 2025 were estimated by management to reach $75 billion, primarily to support AI advancements.

After the DeepSeek-R1 (DEEPSEEK) market panic, many investors were looking for lower capital expenditures from Big Tech companies moving forward, but this is simply not going to be the case. Compute clusters remain the AI moat for now, and most likely will for the indefinite future. $75 billion in capital expenditures for FY25 is a positive for Google shareholders. Google Cloud is currently experiencing capacity constraints in its AI cloud services due to high demand, so ramping up capital expenditures to meet customers' needs is logical. In tandem, it also helps Google to secure itself as one of the dominant Big Tech AI compute infrastructure providers over the long term, with a deeply impenetrable moat related to capex and compute power. This is a long-term win-win for Google's customers and shareholders, despite the short-term impact on free cash flow.

"We do see and have been seeing very strong demand for AI products in the fourth quarter in 2024. We exited the year with more demand than we had available capacity. So we are in a tight supply demand situation, working very hard to bring more capacity online." – Anat Ashkenazi, Chief Financial Officer, Q4 2024 Earnings Call (emphasis added)

I expect continued, stable, moderate growth from Google in FY25. The company has established a rhythm in delivering reliable but moderate alpha to investors; I do not expect this to change in the foreseeable future. However, there are specific catalysts that could help to improve the alpha prospects for Google shareholders. AI-powered Google Search is the catalyst we will be focusing on in detail today.

Google Search Market Position

Despite emerging competitors, Google continues to dominate internet navigation. As of January 2025, it held approximately a 90% share of the worldwide search engine market. Management decided to integrate advanced AI technologies, such as Gemini (its most capable and flexible AI model to date), into Google Search subtly, allowing it to maintain consumer satisfaction without tampering significantly with the world's most-established internet navigation service. If management had taken too aggressive an approach to adapting Google Search too soon, the consumer reaction may very well have proved negative. The 90% share of the worldwide search market attributed to Google does not directly account for the growing reliance on AI models, but recent data suggests that ChatGPT has between a 1% and 5% search market share in 2025, depending on how 'search' is defined. Microsoft's (MSFT) integration of OpenAI's technology into its Bing search engine has also significantly contributed to its 2.18% February 2023 search market share rising to 3.98% in early 2025. Moreover, Google faces increasing regulatory scrutiny concerning its market dominance, with Google Search being investigated for monopoly practices in multiple countries around the world. Therefore, Google Search is being attacked from many angles. Most importantly, the growing threat to Google from AI models providing search functions, most specifically from ChatGPT, should not be underestimated. As the current market adapts and new market share data accrues, I find it likely that Google Search could be relegated to an 80% search market share within the next five to 10 years, primarily due to AI model competitors.

However, Google has a lot of potential to show resilience against this, not least because of its present dominant position. Ultimately, it would be the failure of management to take risks with Google Search in adapting it for the modern age with sufficient aggression, that could set in motion its medium- to long-term decline. To circumnavigate the risk of consumer dissatisfaction with altering traditional Google Search, management can opt for staggered and isolated beta testing to assess the consumer response. While the company has introduced AI Overviews, initially rolled out to a select group of testers in May 2024, this is a somewhat superfluous addition. Instead, what investors should be most excited about is the potential of Search Labs and its current Search Generative Experience; the features are currently only available to customers after being accepted from a waitlist. After reviewing the functionalities, I feel quite confident that once Google hones this Search Generative Experience and rolls it out worldwide, it is going to secure Google as the continued dominant internet search provider as AI models proliferate. OpenAI has a serious threat here to its long-term ambition of chatbot search market accretion, primarily because Google commands a universally accepted brand likely to maintain customer stickiness with a revolutionized, modern Google AI Search experience.

Google AI Search Risks

While Generative Search Experience has a lot of long-term potential, its initial development could prove too overbearing with AI features. A loss of the original Google Search experience could see traditional users defect to Bing. This is why management is acting carefully to ensure a positive outcome regarding user retention and satisfaction. A careful blend that focuses on modernization, efficiency, and accuracy is more important than harnessing all of the advanced features available under a direct use of Google's Gemini, which would disrupt the traditional user-friendly Google Search experience.

In addition, heavier AI-generated Google Search results may mean reduced web traffic to original content sources, which may have an effect on Google's advertising revenue as advertisers reconsider the net benefits of both paid and organic reach on Google Search. However, this could also swing in favor of Google, as if organic reach drops, advertisers may seek to spend more to maintain or increase page views accordingly.

In addition, a 2023 global survey revealed that over half of Gen Z consumers use social media to research brands, compared to 45% relying on search engines. This places Meta in a very strong position to harness much stronger search capabilities as Meta AI scales within its Family of Apps. There is also evidence that this trend is increasing in favor of alternative search methods opposed to Google; data from 2024 shows that 46% of 18-24 year olds begin their online queries on Google, compared to 58% of 25-39 year olds. As an example of popular and distinct internet navigation tools, consider TikTok and Instagram, which focus on visual elements and trusted influencers for a more genuine connection. Google maintains competition here with YouTube, where influencers abound, and where YouTube Shorts acts as a direct competitor to TikTok. However, the risk of market dilution over time for Google remains acute in this respect.

Valuation

Google's cost of equity is calculated as the 10-Year Treasury Constant Maturity Rate plus Google's beta of 0.89 multiplied by a market premium of 6%, equaling 9.818%.

Although the current P/E ratio is below the 200-day moving average, initially indicating a prudent buying opportunity, I expect the P/E ratio to decrease over the next five years as the company's growth rates moderate due to saturation constraints and monopoly concerns from regulatory bodies affecting further aggressive expansion. My terminal multiple of 22.5 in my five-year valuation model reflects this.

Conclusion: Hold

While it's valid to see Google's recent price pullback as a buying opportunity, I have slightly higher standards than that. I'm looking for a relatively certain 15%+ long-term CAGR for a Buy rating, and while Google may achieve that, it's less probable than I'd like at the present valuation. That said, given the company's prudent positioning of Generative Search Experience, which is currently being beta tested, Google can likely continue to dominate internet navigation in the AI era once it has thoroughly reworked Google Search with AI. Therefore, the company's Search and AI products should be monitored closely, as any signs of significantly increased user engagement as a result of new AI-based product remodeling could spark the beginning of a period of more substantial returns for GOOGL & GOOG shareholders. That's if the company also successfully navigates the regulatory probes into its perceived monopoly practices.

data.title: Bumble: Leadership Instability And Revenue Declines
data.description:
No company in the dating-app world is doing well. Match (MTCH) recently reported a sharp decline in Tinder payers while growth at its star app, Hinge, also slowed fiercely. Following suit, Bumble (NASDAQ:BMBL) just reported a drop in paying users as well, as well as a deceleration in revenue growth that signals just how deep the problems in the company run.

Shares of Bumble dropped more than 15% after releasing Q4 results, bringing the stock's losses over the past year to nearly half of its value. In my view, the company's drop still has plenty of room to go.

I last wrote a bearish note on Bumble in November, downgrading the stock from a prior buy rating when it was trading just under $8 per share. Now, with trends worsening for Bumble amid sharp strategic and leadership changes, I'm reiterating my sell rating here. Bumble's list of problems is long, and none are an easy fix.

Amid declining fundamentals, Bumble is bringing back its founder, Whitney Wolfe Herd, as CEO - replacing Lidiane Jones, the current CEO whose prior experience as CEO of Slack had fostered hope that Jones would usher in an era of growth for Bumble. In reality, her tenure lasted barely over one year. In general, I view founder-led companies as a positive: but in my view, even Herd taking over the reins can do little to solve the structural problems for Bumble. We note as well that Bumble's CFO is departing at the end of the first quarter, yet another signal of the leadership chaos that is threatening the company.

For me, the core red flags for Bumble are:

  • The dating app space is under pressure. As evidenced as well by results from Match, dating apps are struggling to keep users engaged on their apps. User quality has been a major issue: Match recently instituted new policies that upped the requirements for account creation, with the hopes of increasing the veracity and quality of its member pool - but the result has been a sharp drop in paid users for Tinder. Bumble is undergoing major platform changes as well, introducing a new feature where men can now initiate the conversation - which will perhaps detract from Bumble's unique factor.
  • Weaker market position. Amid a tough time for the industry overall, Bumble already lags behind many more popular dating apps like Tinder and Hinge in terms of users. In addition, its subsidiary Badoo is a drag on the company as its popularity fades beneath many more mainstream apps.
  • Underperforming subsidiaries. Back when times were good for Bumble, the company also attempted to rival Match by building a portfolio of acquired dating apps as well - but now the company is shuttering several of its underperforming properties, Fruitz and Official. Pooling development resources into Bumble won't necessarily help to reinvigorate user activity, however.

Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Bumble's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The key headline is that Bumble lost paid users, reversing a multi-quarter trend of adding users:

The company ended Q4 with 2.81 million paid users, losing 56k users sequentially since Q3. This compares to net adds of 52k and 87k in Q3 and Q4, respectively.

It's useful to note how Bumble is stacking up against the competition as well. In the same quarter, Tinder fared even worse with a loss of 454k paid users (or ~5% of its paid user base sequentially relative to Q3, versus a ~2% loss for Bumble).

Bumble is, however, faring worse than Hinge, which added 17k paid users in Q4 - though that's a much slower pace than 118k adds in Q3 and 60k adds in Q2. The key point here is that it appears the whole dating app space took a hit on paid users in Q4 - but while Hinge is still growing, Tinder and Bumble are falling behind.

Bumble, however, has a problem that Match does not: at the same time that Bumble is losing paid users, the average revenue per paying user is also falling quite dramatically, down to $25.17 in Q4 - an -8% y/y decline. The core driver here is the fact that Bumble's user mix is increasingly tilting overseas, where both lower local currency pricing (versus the U.S.) as well as a strong U.S. dollar are weighing on the company's average revenue per user. Taken together, however, Bumble's revenue declined -4% y/y in Q4 - three points worse than a -1% y/y decline in Q3.

Worse yet, the company is expecting trends to get even though. The company's revenue guidance of between $242-$248 million for Q1 implies an -8% y/y decline.

With such poor performance, it's fairly evident why the company's board thought this to be an appropriate moment for a wholesale leadership shake-up. We note as well that on top of the aforementioned CEO and CFO change, the company recently also let its CMO go in January - which is unsurprising after a cheeky marketing campaign earlier in 2024 triggered immense customer backlash, and is likely responsible for at least some of the drain in paid users counts.

The slightly positive offset here is that, at the very least, Bumble has managed to maintain operating expense discipline in a tough business environment. The chart above showcases that Bumble's adjusted EBITDA, though almost flat on a nominal basis y/y at $72.5 million, improved by 80bps to a 27.7% margin. As a reminder, the company let go of more than one-third of its corporate headcount earlier in 2024 - though the fact that this mass-scale layoff was only barely enough to offset revenue declines showcases how deep the company's revenue problems go.

Bumble remains indebted, but at relatively manageable levels. Its latest balance sheet showcases cash of $204.3 million and debt at $617.1 million, or a net debt position of $412.8 million - a 1.4x leverage ratio against its FY24 adjusted EBITDA.

Valuation and key takeaways

Now, there's no doubt that Bumble's share price declines over the past year have left the stock a unique value play in an otherwise expensive stock market. But the question for investors is: is it really worth investing in Bumble for price alone, when it has so many fundamental challenges to surmount?

At post-earnings share prices below $7, Bumble trades at a market cap of $719.4 million. After adding back the company's $412.8 million of net debt, its resulting enterprise value is $1.13 billion.

Bumble only guides one quarter out at a time, but Wall Street consensus is expecting the company to generate $1.05 billion in revenue in FY25, representing a -2% y/y decline. If we assume a flat adjusted EBITDA margin profile (28.4% margin) on that revenue, adjusted EBITDA for the year would be $298.2 million (-2% y/y). This puts Bumble's valuation multiples at:

  • 1.1x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 3.8x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA

However, with revenue declines slated to worsen to the high single digits in Q1 (and potentially cause Bumble to miss the Street's low-single digit decline consensus), Bumble stock remains a risky value trap. Until we see signs of a turnaround in paid users in revenue, I'm more comfortable staying on the sidelines here.

data.title: Genuine Parts: Long-Term Upside Still Here, Buy Confirmed
data.description:
While the market waits for a genuine sales growth inflection, we see further opportunities for Genuine Parts' (NYSE:GPC) equity story. The company just released its Q4 results, and we will report a few positive takeaways to consider.

Since our double-down, GPC's stock price has increased by more than 10% (Fig 1). Regarding our buy rating status, we backed the company for the following reasons: 1) GPC's valuation compared to peers, 2) supportive capital allocation priorities, and 3) a story of solid execution. Additionally, on a higher level, we continue to report a positive correlation between increased car complexity & an aging car population in auto parts companies' sales evolution.

Fig 1

Q4 Results and Our Positive Take

GPC top-line sales increased by 3.3% to $5.8 billion compared to last year (Fig 2). The company's Q4 headlines were slightly better than Wall Street market expectations; however, GPC's 2025 outlook is around the market's bar, and does not include details to its pension plan, which could push its EPS lower. The company's sales benefitted from the acquisition and a favorable FX. Additionally, an extra selling day contributed 110 basis points to its 3.3% sales growth, which we believe likely contributed to the gross margin leverage in Q4. Gross profit reached $2.1 billion; a 1.8% increase compared to Q4 2023.

By division, GPC's Auto segment EBITDA margin was down 100 basis points year-on-year to 7.8%, and GPC's Industrial segment EBITDA margin decreased by 40 basis points to 12.9%. Moving forward, it will be crucial for Genuine Parts to focus on execution to ensure it does not need to lower its outlook. Going down to the P&L analysis, GPC's adjusted net income reached $224 million, with a $1.61 EPS. In the adjustment EPS, the company does not report restructuring costs, which will continue to be reported as a non-recurring expense also in 2025.

On the balance sheet, the company's inventory increased by 17.9% year over year. The company reached an operating cash flow of $1.3 billion, which was insufficient to cover investing activities and CAPEX, $1.1 billion and $567 million invested, respectively. For this reason, GPC's debt is slightly higher than the 2023 results. That said, the company has a strong capital structure with a total debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio of 2.2x.

Fig 2

Why are we positive?

  1. On a higher level, looking at the automotive companies' readouts, we report that Porsche (OTCPK:POAHY) is back to invest in new Internal Combustion Engine [ICE] models for €800 million. Even if GPC and P911 are not directly correlated, this is a positive sign of car complexity. There is some positive news that the EU EV adoption rate is significantly slowing down; we believe it will be an ICE sale ban by 2035. We very much like the BMW CEO approach that "raised concerns about the risks tied to the EU's reliance on Chinese EV battery manufacturers and stressed the importance of a more flexible regulatory approach that embraces multiple low-emission technologies;"
  2. Looking at the peers' readouts on a US development, we report Advance Auto's announcement to withdraw from several markets. This is positive for the other players in the space. The company is moving from Washington, California, Oregon, Arizona, and Nevada and will close some underperforming stores in different markets. This will reduce the number of stores located in the city by almost 700. We believe that as commercial customers are informed of the closure of their local Advance store, these clients will look to shift their business to supply products. GPC with NAPA might really benefit from this announcement. According to our calculation, Advance Auto's average sales stores produced approximately $1 million in sales. Napa has approximately 12% sales of its USA stores in these states, and assuming 20/30% of higher market share penetration, this could imply $175 million of additional sales;
  3. In the last quarter, GPC's gross margin increased by ~49 basis points to 36.9%, with the company's adjusted SG&A deleveraged by ~210 basis points. This translates into a higher core operating profit of $172 million. Looking ahead, the company still projects an annualized saving of $200 million per year thanks to its internal restructuring target. This will be viewed positively by Wall Street;
  4. Still related to GPC capital priorities, we must report a 3% DPS increase. "The company has paid a cash dividend every year since going public in 1948, and 2025 marks the 69th consecutive year of increased dividends paid to shareholders."

Earnings Changes and Valuation

In our last assessment, we arrived at a 2024 turnover estimate of $23.2 billion. The company's sales reached $23.4 billion. Looking to 2025, we see the company taking a conservative stance on the top line, with an H2-weighted recovery in both segments. So, we implied a 2% sales growth in both segments thanks to acquisitions/and the Advance Auto announcement and considering FX pressure. Assuming a gross margin evolution aligned with Q4 results, intangible asset amortization up by low-single digits, and restructuring expense of $165 million, this would translate into a core operating profit of 6.6%, with a decline of 20 basis points compared to 2024 results. With a 24% tax rate, our net income reached $1.1 billion with an EPS of $8.1. The company guides an adjusted EPS between $7.75 - $8.25 (Fig 3) with approximately $0.30 EPS headwind on partially mitigating its pension plan.

Without looking at GPC's closest peers (O'Reilly Automotive (ORLY), Advance Auto Parts (AAP), and AutoZone (AZO)), we continue to value the company with an unchanged P/E multiple of 16x. Following a lower EPS projection, we lower the GPC target price from $132.8 to $129.6. We report that GPC has a double-digit EBITDA margin with an ROIC of above 15%. Despite a lower price, we confirmed our buy rating estimates on a twelve-month view. We also report that the PC P/E target is below its stock's historical averages (Fig 4) but warranted, in our view, given the lagged cyclical recovery in the industrial segment. We also note the core holder of the company is an equity income/dividend growth fund, which is most devoted to cash flow sustainability and dividend outlook. GPC scores highly in that regard, which is a positive sign.

Fig 3

Fig 4

Risks

We have covered GPC since 2022, so we report the following downside risks: 1) changes in consumer spending with a slower-than-anticipated 2025 industrial recovery (that said, with a Trump second mandate, GPC might benefit from industrial onshoring activities), 2) declines in the average age of vehicles or miles driven in the EU and USA, 3) higher competition with online channel shift, 4) failure in accretive acquisitions, 5) higher inventory as reported in Q4. On a new risk, we report FX changes and uncertainty on GPC's new pension plan.

Conclusion

We priced GPC based on a 12-month view and not on its long-term growth potential. Still, the company screens well on balance sheet ratio and dividend coverage. We are optimistic about the complexity of cars and the aging fleet in GPC's main markets. This is still a buy.

data.title: Tracking Seth Klarman's Baupost Group Holdings - Q4 2024 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Baupost Group's 13F stock portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on Klarman’s regulatory 13F Form filed on 02/13/2025. Please visit our Tracking Seth Klarman’s Baupost Group Holdings article for an idea on how his holdings have progressed over the years and our previous update for the fund's moves during Q3 2024.

Baupost Group’s 13F portfolio value decreased from $3.52B to $3.43B this quarter. The total number of 13F securities increased from 21 to 22. The portfolio is heavily concentrated with Liberty Global, Willis Towers Watson, Alphabet, WESCO, and CRH plc together accounting for ~56% of the 13F holdings.

Since inception (1982), Baupost Group’s 13F portfolio has accounted for between 2.4% to 15% of the Assets Under Management (AUM). The current allocation is at the high-end of that range. The rest of the AUM is diversified among cash, debt, real estate, and hedges. On average, the fund has held ~25% cash over the last decade. Seth Klarman’s distinct investment style is elaborated in his 1991 book “Margin of Safety: Risk-averse value investing strategies for the thoughtful investor”. The book is out-of-print and copies sell for a huge premium.

New Stakes:

Ferguson Enterprises (FERG): FERG is a fairly large 5.72% of the portfolio position purchased this quarter at prices between $173.58 and $221.78. The stock currently trades at ~$182.

Restaurant Brands (QSR): The 5.51% QSR stake was established this quarter at prices between $64.91 and $72.84 and the stock is now just below that range at $63.19.

Sunrise Communications (SNRE): SNRE is a ~3% portfolio position that came about as a result of the spinoff from Liberty Global. It started trading in November 2024 at $47 per share, and it is now at $46.38.

Humana Inc. (HUM): The 2.56% HUM stake was established this quarter at prices between $233.08 and $303.12 and the stock currently trades at ~$266.

Genuine Parts (GPC), and Liberty Broadband (LBRDK): These very small (less than ~1% of the portfolio each) stakes were established this quarter.

Stake Disposals:

Fidelity National Information Services (FIS): The 2.31% FIS position was purchased during Q3 2022 at prices between ~$76 and ~$105. There was a ~250% stake increase during Q4 2022 at prices between ~$57 and ~$85. That was followed with a ~125% increase in the next quarter at prices between ~$50 and ~$78. Q2 2023 also saw a ~40% increase at prices between ~$52 and ~$59. Q2 2024 saw a ~37% selling at prices between $66.89 and $78. That was followed by a ~72% reduction during the last quarter at prices between $72.48 and $85.04. The disposal this quarter was at prices between $80.14 and $90.57. The stock is now at $69.42.

Jacobs Solutions (J): Jacobs Solutions was a 1.31% of the portfolio stake established during H2 2023 at prices between ~$119 and ~$140. The stake was decreased by 30% in the next quarter at prices between ~$126 and ~$154. The last quarter saw a similar reduction at prices between ~$114 and ~$130. The elimination this quarter was at prices between $131.80 and $149.25. The stock currently trades at ~$130.

Fortrea Holdings (FTRE): The 1.30% FTRE position was primarily built during Q2 2024 at prices between $22.48 and $40.28. The last quarter saw a ~27% selling at prices between $19.48 and $27.92. The stake was sold this quarter at prices between $16.82 and $23.73. The stock currently trades at $15.39.

Stake Increases:

Willis Towers Watson (WTW): WTW is a large (top three) ~17% of the portfolio stake established in Q1 2021 at prices between ~$200 and ~$235. The two quarters through Q3 2021 saw a ~60% selling at prices between ~$202 and ~$270 while the next quarter there was a ~25% stake increase at prices between ~$226 and ~$249. There was a similar reduction in Q2 2022 at prices between ~$191 and ~$243. Q3 2022 saw a ~40% increase at prices between ~$192 and ~$221 while during Q1 2023 there was a one-third reduction at prices between ~$220 and ~$257. Q3 2023 saw a ~72% stake increase at prices between ~$196 and ~$234. The stock currently trades at ~$326. The last few quarters also saw minor increases.

WESCO International (WCC): The ~6% WCC position was purchased during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$133 and ~$194. The next quarter saw the stake built at prices between ~$152 and ~$191. There was a ~55% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$149 and ~$183. That was followed by a ~14%stake increase this quarter at prices between $163.76 and $212.73. The stock is now at ~$197.

CRH plc (CRH): The large (top five) ~7% CRH stake was primarily built during Q3 2023 at prices between ~$53 and ~$60. The stake was decreased by 19% during Q1 2024 at prices between $65.92 and $87.20. The next quarter saw a ~55% stake increase at prices between $72.72 and $86.02. The last quarter saw a ~38% reduction at prices between $71.76 and $93.24. The stock currently trades at ~$106. There was marginal increase this quarter.

Eagle Materials (EXP): EXP is a 4.59% of the portfolio stake established during Q1 2024 at prices between ~$196 and ~$272. The next quarter saw a ~30% stake increase at prices between ~$213 and ~$274. The last quarter saw a ~45% stake increase at prices between ~$212 and ~$292. That was followed by a ~28% stake increase this quarter at prices between $246.48 and $315. The stock currently trades at ~$240.

Herbalife (HLF): The 1.48% HLF position was increased by 34% during Q1 2024 at prices between $8.03 and $15.36. That was followed by a ~130% stake increase during the next quarter at prices between $7.65 and $11.70. This quarter saw another ~50% stake increase at prices between $6.33 and $8.67. The stock is now below their purchase price ranges at $5.62.

Note: They have a ~9% ownership stake in the business.

Stake Decreases:

Liberty Global (LBTYK): LBTYK is currently the largest position at ~17% of the portfolio. It was established in Q3 2018 at prices between ~$15 and ~$19 and increased by ~120% next quarter at prices between ~$10 and ~$18. There was another ~27% stake increase in Q1 2019 at prices between ~$10 and ~$16. Since then, the activity had been minor. There was a ~10% reduction in Q2 2022 at prices between ~$12 and ~$16. The two quarters through Q3 2023 saw another ~10% trimming. This quarter also saw a ~10% trimming. The stock currently trades at $11.54.

Note: The prices quoted above are adjusted for the Sunrise (SNRE) spinoff in November.

Alphabet Inc. (GOOG): The ~8% GOOG stake was purchased in Q1 2020 at prices between ~$53 and ~$76. The last three quarters of 2020 had seen a ~75% selling at prices between ~$55 and ~$92. There was a ~265% stake increase in Q1 2021 at prices between ~$86 and ~$105. Q4 2021 saw a ~22% trimming at prices between ~$133 and ~$151 while next quarter there was a ~7% increase. The zigzag trading pattern continued in the next quarter: ~30% reduction at prices between ~$106 and ~$144. That was followed with another ~60% selling during Q3 2022 at prices between ~$96 and ~$123. The stake was rebuilt next quarter at prices between ~$83.50 and ~$105. Q1 2023 saw a ~47% stake increase at prices between ~$87 and ~$109 while the next quarter saw a ~30% selling at prices between ~$104 and ~$128. There was another ~23% reduction during Q4 2023 at prices between ~$123 and ~$143. Q2 2024 also saw a ~65% selling at prices between ~$152 and ~$187. There was a ~37% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$150 and ~$192. The stock is now at ~$187. There was a minor ~3% trimming this quarter.

Clarivate Plc (CLVT): The 5.37% of the portfolio CLVT position was established during Q3 2023 at prices between $6.60 and $9.90. There was a ~13% stake increase in the next quarter. That was followed by a ~20% increase during Q1 2024 at prices between $7.01 and $9.44. The next quarter saw another ~27% stake increase at prices between $5.32 and $7.65. The stock currently trades below their purchase price ranges at $4.76. There was a minor ~7% trimming this quarter.

Note: They have a ~5.2% ownership stake in the business.

Dollar General (DG): DG is a 4.55% of the portfolio position purchased during the last quarter at prices between ~$80 and ~$132 and the stock currently trades below that range at $75.63. There was a ~11% trimming this quarter at prices between $72.70 and $83.23.

Solventum Corp. (SOLV): The 2.78% SOLV stake established during Q2 2024 at prices between $50.75 and $70.06. There was a ~20% selling this quarter at prices between $66.05 and $75.07. The stock is now at $73.98.

Kept Steady:

Viasat (VSAT): VSAT is a ~3% of the portfolio position. Klarman first purchased VSAT in 2008 at a cost-basis in the high-teens. In July 2020, Baupost participated in Viasat’s 4.47M share private placement by acquiring ~2.56M shares at ~$39 per share. The last quarter saw a ~11% selling at prices between $11.94 and $24.49. The stock currently trades at $9.32.

Note: They have a ~10% ownership stake in the business.

GDS Holdings (GDS): The 2.94% GDS stake saw a ~65% stake increase during Q2 2024 at prices between $6.11 and $9.75. The stock currently trades at $43.01.

The spreadsheet below highlights changes to Klarman's 13F stock holdings in Q4 2024:

Source: John Vincent. Data constructed from Baupost Group’s 13F filings for Q3 2024 and Q4 2024.

data.title: UnitedHealth Group: Mid-Teens EPS Growth Trumps The Risks
data.description:
The Health Care sector has been the worst of the 11 S&P 500 areas in the past 12 months, but we've seen some periods of modest relative strength in the last three months. Moreover, among small caps, Health Care is actually the best sector so far in 2025. Indeed, there has been some weak performance among the large-cap Health Care names since the middle of last year, including shares of UnitedHealth Group (NYSE:UNH) (NEOE:UNH:CA).

I was bullish on the stock throughout last year, and I reiterate a sanguine stance despite plenty of macro and fiscal uncertainties. The $482 billion market cap Managed Health Care industry company has dealt with firm-specific issues lately, too. We've seen the largest component of the Dow Jones Industrial Average struggle through the first half of the first quarter, widening the gap to fair value in my opinion.

What's more, the technical chart is now approaching key long-term support after breaching an area I noted in my previous analysis.

Health Care: Worst S&P 500 Sector YoY, But Best Among Small Caps YTD

Back in January, UNH reported a mixed set of quarterly results. Q4 non-GAAP EPS of $6.81 beat the Wall Street consensus forecast of $6.74 while revenue of $101 billion, up 6.8% from the same period a year earlier, was a significant $930 million miss. Shares fell 6% in the session that followed, making it back-to-back poor earnings reactions.

Ahead of the April report, the options market prices in a material 4.8% earnings-related stock price swing based on the straddle pricing-that's a full percentage point above the long-term implied move, so we could see another large reaction.

As for its Q4, UNH once again missed on the street's Medical Loss Ratio (MLR) estimate. The figure verified at 87.6%, which was also above UNH's own forecast. The firm's member mix, Medicaid reimbursements, and costs related to medications were drivers of the high metric. For reference, the MLR is like a health insurance company's profit margin, only inverted.

Still, the management team affirmed its FY 2025 revenue and earnings guidance in January; at the time, it saw $450-$455 billion on the top line and adjusted EPS to be in the range of $29.50-$30. The onus is now on UNH to continue to outperform its peers on execution and keeping costs in check.

Another weak spot was the margin story with OptumHealth, but other units picked up the slack. Overall, margins should be a bright spot this year, but a lot will hinge on policy changes, and the company hopes to improve on its FY 2024 MLR of 85.5%.

On the earnings outlook, it's steady as she goes in terms of operating EPS. The consensus calls for almost $30 of non-GAAP per-share earnings in the current year, with strong 12-14% of outyear through FY 2026 EPS gains. That makes today's mid-high teens price-to-earnings ratio compelling in my view.

The sellside has turned less upbeat, however, largely due to the tragedy in January and unease about what the Trump administration may do with healthcare policy and DOGE. But profitability trends are strong, evidenced by UNH's high 4.4% free cash flow yield.

UnitedHealth: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, now into FY 2025, if we assume operating EPS of $30.50 over the next 12 months and apply a 19x P/E, then shares should trade near $580.

I lowered my earnings multiple assumption by 1.6 figures due to potential action against major US health insurance companies in the new backdrop and after the January murder of Brian Thompson. That still leaves UNH materially undervalued, so I retain the buy rating.

UNH: Broadly Favorable Valuation Metrics

Key risks for UNH include higher MLRs industrywide if healthcare utilization increases, weaker growth in Optum, and the aforementioned policy uncertainty with RFK, Jr at the helm of the Department of Health and Human Services.

The company also exposed patients' data to hackers earlier this year, so cybersecurity challenges should be acknowledged.

Competitor Analysis

I also wanted to mention that seasonality is quite bullish for UNH shares right now. The March through July period has historically produced strong returns when analyzing trends over the past 10 years.

UNH: Bullish Calendar Trends

Looking ahead, corporate event risk data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q1 2025 earnings date of Tuesday, April 15 BMO. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With the stock still undervalued in my estimation, UNH's technical situation has turned worse. Notice in the chart below that shares have fallen back below the key $555 mark I detailed last year. Now, with a high amount of volume by price right near where UNH trades, it is a battle between the bulls and the bears. The long-term 200-day moving average, while it is rising in its slope, is above price, which the longs never want to see.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph - it printed a bearish divergence to price when UNH notched a new all-time high in November. The good news is that with a steep drop just in the last few weeks, UNH is not far from major long-term support beginning at $475.

UNH: Shares Back Below Resistance, Long-Term Support in the Mid-$400s

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on UNH. Amid poor absolute and relative price action, the Health Care stalwart appears compelling on valuation ahead of a very bullish seasonal period.

data.title: Cryptocurrencies: Bitcoin Inches Down To 5-Week Low
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

This weekly update tracks some of the largest cryptocurrencies by market share: bitcoin (BTC-USD) and ether (ETH-USD). While both are considered to be high risk when it comes to investing, the two have foundational differences that investors should know.

We've also included XRP (XRP-USD), as it was one of the largest cryptocurrencies when this series began. According to Wikipedia, a cryptocurrency is "a digital asset designed to work as a medium of exchange that uses cryptography to secure its transactions, to control the creation of additional units, and to verify the transfer of assets."

Bitcoin

Bitcoin was the world's first cryptocurrency and decentralized digital currency. The first bitcoin transaction occurred in early 2009 and has since grown worldwide to a mainstream financial asset. It is often considered volatile, as seen in our first chart, but one can argue that it is also resilient.

Bitcoin's closing price edged lower, settling just above $95,500 - its lowest level in over five weeks. BTC remains up ~1% year to date, but is now ~10% below its record high.

Ether

Ether is a cryptocurrency run on the Ethereum blockchain platform and was launched in July 2015. It has the second-largest market share, despite being the newest of the three discussed in this article.

Ether's closing price ticked higher this week, but ETH remains down ~20% year to date and ~45% below its all-time high.

XRP

XRP, which is owned by Ripple, was launched in 2012 and was one of the larger cryptocurrencies for some time until new coins joined the market.

XRP surged during the last few months of 2024 and the during the first month of 2025. However, it pulled back in February and is now up only ~10% year to date.

Bitcoin vs. Ether vs. XRP

An index has been created in order to chart these three cryptocurrencies together, considering their significantly different pricing histories. The chart tells us which cryptocurrency's price has changed the most since November 9, 2017. At one point or another, all three have been at the top. At the time of writing, bitcoin is in the lead.

On January 10th, 2024, the SEC approved spot bitcoin ETFs from a range of issuers such as Grayscale Bitcoin Trust ETF (GBTC), iShares Bitcoin Trust (IBIT), Fidelity Wise Origin Bitcoin Fund (FBTC), ARK 21Shares Bitcoin ETF (ARKB), Bitwise Bitcoin ETF (BITB), Coinshares Valkyrie Bitcoin Fund (BRRR). Here are some of the quick takeaways from the spot bitcoin ETF launch.

On July 23rd, 2024, a handful of spot ether ETFs launched from a range of issuers such as Grayscale Ethereum Trust (ETHE), Franklin Ethereum ETF (EZET), Bitwise Ethereum ETF (ETHW), iShares Ethereum Trust (ETHA), Fidelity Ethereum ETF (FETH).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Reinsurance Group: Baby Bonds Offer A Solid Return In Either Outcome
data.description:
Reinsurance Group of America Inc. (NYSE:RGA) has been hitting it out of the park. The largest life reinsurance company had an extremely challenging time with the pandemic. Its earnings contracted over 50% from 2019 to 2020 and were down over 80% from its 2017 numbers. But through that trough came higher reinsurance rates and, well, higher interest rates. The first ensured profitability in its base business, and the latter enhanced the yield earned on its float. In the most recent quarter, RGA demonstrated just how powerful these tailwinds were.

With over $22 per share of earnings, the company has more than tripled its pandemic year numbers. Estimates for the next 3 years look good, though RGA may be at the peak of the rate cycle and earnings are unlikely to grow much.

Outlook For RGA

RGA and other insurance stocks look relatively cheap if you focus on price to earnings ratios. Most of them are sporting a low P/E ratio and in RGA's case it is just about 8.5X. Historically, we have paid more attention to price to tangible book values as they are a better indicator pick trades in this sector. When this metric is high, it means that the company is in the midst of an upcycle in rates and investors are forgetting that rates come down eventually. When this metric is low, it means that despondency rules the day and one can look for a buying opportunity. Where are we today? Based on that logic, RGA looks actually expensive and would normally merit a sell rating from us at 1.22X tangible book value multiple.

But we have to adjust that based on one major factor. Where interest rates are headed. Our take here is that RGA and other reinsurance stocks will likely face lowered pricing going forward, and earnings have likely peaked here. On the other hand, the ultra-low price to tangible book values were more suited for the ZIRP (zero Interest rate policy) era. In that era, these stocks only made via their operating profits and did not make much on their float. You can see in the chart above that RGA held that 1.2X multiple on tangible book value from 2004-2007, when interest rates were generally higher than in the ZIRP era. Overall, we come away with a hold (versus a sell) and would generally consider buying this only at a sub 1.1X multiple of tangible book.

The Baby Bonds

Reinsurance Group of America, Incorporated SB DB FX/FL56 (RZB) is something that we had on our watch list for some time, and we added them as low as $24.36. The company is rated A by S&P and junior/subordinated bonds here are rated BBB+. This may not seem like a slam dunk investment since it only has a coupon of 5.75%, but we added it as it was one of the highest quality picks for a short duration. This may seem contradictory to the bond's maturity date, which is in 2056. Well, there is more to this. But first, let us see how things work if the bond is redeemed. As long as you buy it under par, you should get more than 5.75% yield to call. At the $24.36 price point, the yield to call was well over 6.75%. This is pretty good for a sub two-year bond. iShares 1-5 Year Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (IGSB) which focuses on quality at the low end of the duration curve, as an average yield to maturity of just 4.92%.

Plus, you make less than that after fees, and the average bond has a lower rating than this one. So this works get great for a call/redemption. What if the bond is reset on June 15, 2026?

The spread commencing on 6/15/2026 to the maturity date will be equal to three-month LIBOR, reset quarterly, plus 4.04%. The spread was set at a time when LIBOR was all we knew, but the real reset will obviously be adjusted to SOFR. So you will get SOFR+ 4.04% plus a small spread adjustment. The reason we like it is as part of a diversified portfolio is that you do want some shorter term rate exposure. If you have to have that (assuming they don't call it) you would want a really high spread for the quality of the bond. To give a comparative here, we can talk about Annaly Capital Management (NLY) which is a mortgage REIT with 6X leverage has Annaly Capital Management, Inc. PFD SER G (NLY.PR.G) which was issued with a LIBOR spread of just 4.172%. The credit quality of these firms is miles apart. So if you think 4.172% from NLY.PR.G is great, you should love 4.04% from RGA. Based on the market prices, (NLY.PR.G is trading over par), we think RZB is a fairly decent deal here. If our current financial conditions persist, it will be called/redeemed at $25.00 on June 15, 2026. If things get dicey in the markets, it is possible that redemption will be put off. In that case, we are happy picking up this spread relative to risk-free rates.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints.
